# Review: deepmind/973.lean

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` on line 17.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (lines 19–25).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 973`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/973`.
- [ ] **Missing brief plain-English description of the problem in the module docstring.** The problem description appears only in the theorem's docstring (lines 29–36), not in the module docstring itself. The checklist requires the module docstring to include a brief plain-English description. Consider adding a one-sentence summary such as: *"Does there exist a constant C > 1 such that for every n ≥ 2 there is a sequence of complex numbers with modulus ≥ 1, starting at 1, whose power sums are exponentially small?"*
- [x] Bibliography entry present: `[Er65b]` Erdős (1965), _Extremal problems in number theory_, p.213.

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos973 ... end Erdos973`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos973`.
- N/A: No `open` / `open scoped` statements needed.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- N/A: No custom `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword (line 38).
- [x] Main theorem named `erdos_973` — matches namespace number, `snake_case`.
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same** attribute line (line 37): `@[category research open, AMS 30]`.
- [x] Has a docstring (lines 29–36) describing the problem and referencing the source.
- [x] Docstring references source paper: `[Er65b, p.213]` and cross-references problem `[519]`.

### `answer()` usage

The original problem asks "Does there exist a constant C > 1 such that ..." — this is a **yes/no existence question** (Case 1).

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P` — correct for Case 1.
- [x] The RHS formalizes the question as originally asked: existence of C > 1 with the exponential bound on power sums. `answer(True)` would mean "yes, such a C exists" and `answer(False)` would mean "no" — semantics are correct.
- [x] Problem is open, so `answer(sorry)` is appropriate (not yet resolved to `True`/`False`).
- [x] All quantifiers (`∃ C`, `∀ n`, `∃ z`, `∀ i`, `∀ k`) appear **after** `answer(sorry)` on the RHS of `↔`, not as theorem parameters.

**Formalization correctness:** The Lean statement faithfully captures the original problem:
- `∃ C : ℝ, C > 1` — existence of constant C > 1. ✓
- `∀ (n : ℕ) (_ : n ≥ 2)` — for every n ≥ 2. ✓
- `∃ z : Fin n → ℂ` — there exists a sequence z₁, …, zₙ ∈ ℂ. ✓
- `z ⟨0, by omega⟩ = 1` — z₁ = 1 (0-indexed). ✓
- `∀ i, 1 ≤ ‖z i‖` — |zᵢ| ≥ 1 for all i. ✓
- `∀ k : ℕ, 2 ≤ k → k ≤ n + 1 → ‖∑ i : Fin n, z i ^ k‖ < C⁻¹ ^ n` — max₍₂≤k≤n+1₎ |∑ zᵢᵏ| < C⁻ⁿ. The universal quantification over k correctly encodes the max bound. `C⁻¹ ^ n = (C⁻¹)ⁿ = C⁻ⁿ`. ✓

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute present.
- [x] Value is `research open` — valid; the problem remains open per erdosproblems.com.
- [x] `research open` theorem contains `sorry` in its proof body.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute: `AMS 30`.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: 30.
- [x] Numbers in ascending order (single entry — trivially satisfied).
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] Code 30 is a valid MSC2020 code: **30** (Functions of a complex variable). Appropriate for a problem about complex sequences and power sums.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses `by` / `sorry` pattern: `:= by` on line 45, `sorry` indented on line 46. This matches the acceptable multi-line form from the checklist.
- N/A: No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches included.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- N/A: No variants present. The file formalizes a single conjecture.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `ℝ`, `ℂ`, `ℕ`, `∀`, `∃`, `≥`, `≤`, `∑`, `‖`, `←`, `↔`, `∧`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all well under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` not run as part of this review. No obvious issues; the only expected warning is `sorry` in the proof of `erdos_973`.
- [ ] Custom linter compliance not verified by build, but manual inspection shows conformance with all five linters:
  - `linter.style.copyright` — correct header.
  - `linter.style.category_attribute` — single valid category.
  - `linter.style.ams_attribute` — single valid AMS tag, ordered, no duplicates.
  - `linter.style.answer_attribute` — `answer(sorry)` used correctly with `↔`; no quantifiers as theorem parameters.
  - `linter.style.namespace` — all declarations inside `Erdos973`.

---

**Verdict:** Minor issue. One checklist item is not fully satisfied: the module docstring (§3) lacks a brief plain-English description of the problem — the description appears only in the theorem docstring. All other mandatory items pass. The formalization is mathematically faithful to the original problem statement.

/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 973

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/973](https://www.erdosproblems.com/973)

[Er65b] Erdős, P., _Extremal problems in number theory_ (1965), p.213.
-/

namespace Erdos973

/--
Does there exist a constant $C > 1$ such that, for every $n \geq 2$, there
exists a sequence $z_1, \ldots, z_n \in \mathbb{C}$ with $z_1 = 1$ and
$|z_i| \geq 1$ for all $i$, such that
$$\max_{2 \leq k \leq n+1} \left| \sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i^k \right| < C^{-n}?$$

A problem of Erdős [Er65b, p.213]. See also [519].
-/
@[category research open, AMS 30]
theorem erdos_973 : answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 1 ∧
    ∀ (n : ℕ) (_ : n ≥ 2),
    ∃ z : Fin n → ℂ,
      z ⟨0, by omega⟩ = 1 ∧
      (∀ i, 1 ≤ ‖z i‖) ∧
      ∀ k : ℕ, 2 ≤ k → k ≤ n + 1 →
        ‖∑ i : Fin n, z i ^ k‖ < C⁻¹ ^ n := by
  sorry

end Erdos973

    33→
    34→## 2. Import Statement

    49→  ```

    68→- [ ] Consider adding `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks for new definitions to verify they behave as expected.

file.content:
# Formal Conjectures PR Checklist

Every item must pass before a PR is approved. Reviewers: reject if any box is unchecked
without an explicit justification.

Sources: [`formal-conjectures/README.md`](../formal-conjectures/README.md) (Style Guidelines §1–7),
[`AGENTS.md`](../formal-conjectures/AGENTS.md), and the five custom linters in
`FormalConjectures/Util/`.

---

## 1. Copyright Header

- [ ] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line):
  ```
  /-
  Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

  Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
  you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
  You may obtain a copy of the License at

      https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

  Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
  distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
  WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
  See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
  limitations under the License.
  -/
  ```
- [ ] Year is exactly `2026` (the linter accepts any 4-digit year, but new files must use the current year).

## 2. Import Statement

- [ ] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line):
  ```
  import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
  ```
- [ ] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [ ] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [ ] Starts with a heading: `# Erdos Problem N` (or appropriate title).
- [ ] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to the source, e.g.:
  ```
  *Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/N](https://www.erdosproblems.com/N)
  ```
- [ ] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
- [ ] Optional: bibliography entries for related papers.

## 4. Namespace

- [ ] All declarations are inside a namespace (`namespace ErdosN ... end ErdosN`).
  - Linter `linter.style.namespace` enforces this; error: *"The declaration '...' is not inside a namespace."*
- [ ] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase` (e.g., `Erdos42`, not `erdos_42`).
- [ ] `open` / `open scoped` statements appear **before** the namespace block when opening Mathlib namespaces (e.g., `open Filter Nat Real`).

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [ ] Helper `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations have a LaTeX docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [ ] Definitions that involve classical choice or nonconstructive operations are marked `noncomputable`.
- [ ] No `sorry` in definitions (only in theorem proof bodies).
- [ ] Types and Props use `UpperCamelCase` (e.g., `IsCoveringSystem`).
- [ ] Functions returning types use `lowerCamelCase` (e.g., `normalizedPrimeGap`).
- [ ] Use `local notation` for problem-specific notation, scoped inside the namespace.
- [ ] Consider adding `@[category test]` or `@[category API]` sanity checks for new definitions to verify they behave as expected.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [ ] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` or `lemma` keyword (not `def` or `example`).
- [ ] Main theorem is named `erdos_N` (snake_case, matching the namespace number).
- [ ] Name follows Lean/Mathlib conventions: `snake_case` for terms of Props.
- [ ] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` attributes on the **same attribute line** (e.g., `@[category research open, AMS 11]`).
- [ ] Main theorems and non-obvious variants have a docstring (`/-- ... -/`).
- [ ] Theorem docstrings should ideally reference the source paper(s) (e.g., `[Er95]`, `[AhKh97]`).

### `answer()` usage

input.new_string:
*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/973](https://www.erdosproblems.com/973)

Does there exist a constant $C > 1$ such that for every $n \geq 2$ there is a sequence of complex
numbers $z_1, \ldots, z_n$ with $z_1 = 1$ and $|z_i| \geq 1$, whose power sums satisfy
$\max_{2 \leq k \leq n+1} |\sum z_i^k| < C^{-n}$?

[Er65b] Erdős, P., _Extremal problems in number theory_ (1965), p.213.

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erdős Problem 973

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/973](https://www.erdosproblems.com/973)

[Er65b] Erdős, P., _Extremal problems in number theory_ (1965), p.213.
-/

namespace Erdos973

/--
Does there exist a constant $C > 1$ such that, for every $n \geq 2$, there
exists a sequence $z_1, \ldots, z_n \in \mathbb{C}$ with $z_1 = 1$ and
$|z_i| \geq 1$ for all $i$, such that
$$\max_{2 \leq k \leq n+1} \left| \sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i^k \right| < C^{-n}?$$

A problem of Erdős [Er65b, p.213]. See also [519].
-/
@[category research open, AMS 30]
theorem erdos_973 : answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 1 ∧
    ∀ (n : ℕ) (_ : n ≥ 2),
    ∃ z : Fin n → ℂ,
      z ⟨0, by omega⟩ = 1 ∧
      (∀ i, 1 ≤ ‖z i‖) ∧
      ∀ k : ℕ, 2 ≤ k → k ≤ n + 1 →
        ‖∑ i : Fin n, z i ^ k‖ < C⁻¹ ^ n := by
  sorry

end Erdos973

+numbers $z_1, \ldots, z_n$ with $z_1 = 1$ and $|z_i| \geq 1$, whose power sums satisfy
[Er65b] Erdős, P., _Extremal problems in number theory_ (1965), p.213.
